items.prompt: A learner reviews a bounded AP-style performance task or project proposal that combines code, data, systems, and impact claims. Show how they would reason across classes, methods, objects, and algorithms in one bounded program explanation.
items.expected_evidence: reason across classes, methods, objects, and algorithms in one bounded program explanation
items.scoring_guidance: Meets when the learner demonstrates reason across classes, methods, objects, and algorithms in one bounded program explanation with bounded and technically valid computing reasoning.

items.prompt: A class is evaluating an object-oriented or multi-component program design for an AP-style task. Show how the learner would apply object-oriented design and algorithm tradeoff thinking together.
items.expected_evidence: apply object-oriented design and algorithm tradeoff thinking together
items.scoring_guidance: Meets when the learner demonstrates apply object-oriented design and algorithm tradeoff thinking together with bounded and technically valid computing reasoning.

items.prompt: A capstone team proposes a cross-domain computing project with stated users, constraints, and architecture choices. Show how the learner would use testing or maintainability evidence when judging an implementation.
items.expected_evidence: use testing or maintainability evidence when judging an implementation
items.scoring_guidance: Meets when the learner demonstrates use testing or maintainability evidence when judging an implementation with bounded and technically valid computing reasoning.

items.prompt: Why is AP CSA understanding weaker when it is reduced to syntax memorization?
items.expected_evidence: because strong AP CSA reasoning integrates object design, algorithmic implementation, and code-quality judgment rather than isolated language recall.
items.scoring_guidance: Meets when the learner explains the structure coherently and ties the explanation back to because strong ap csa reasoning integrates object design, algorithmic implementation, and code-quality judgment rather than isolated language recall.

items.prompt: How should class design connect to algorithmic implementation?
items.expected_evidence: the classes and methods should organize the responsibilities needed to carry out the bounded algorithm, not sit beside it as unrelated structure.
items.scoring_guidance: Meets when the learner explains the structure coherently and ties the explanation back to the classes and methods should organize the responsibilities needed to carry out the bounded algorithm, not sit beside it as unrelated structure.

items.prompt: During an AP-style synthesis or project review, a learner gives a response that suggests this breakdown: learner treats ap csa as syntax or api memorization without integrated design and algorithm reasoning. Diagnose the integration mistake. Focus on syntax-first AP reasoning.
items.expected_evidence: reason across classes, methods, objects, and algorithms in one bounded program explanation
items.scoring_guidance: Meets when the learner identifies the dominant breakdown, names the structural issue accurately, and redirects the work toward reason across classes, methods, objects, and algorithms in one bounded program explanation.

items.prompt: During an AP-style synthesis or project review, a learner gives a response that suggests this breakdown: learner cannot connect class design and object behavior to the algorithm being implemented. Diagnose the integration mistake. Focus on object-algorithm disconnect.
items.expected_evidence: apply object-oriented design and algorithm tradeoff thinking together
items.scoring_guidance: Meets when the learner identifies the dominant breakdown, names the structural issue accurately, and redirects the work toward apply object-oriented design and algorithm tradeoff thinking together.

items.prompt: A learner moves from an AP-style classroom prompt to an authentic design-review or project-planning case and must still use testing or maintainability evidence when judging an implementation.
items.expected_evidence: use testing or maintainability evidence when judging an implementation
items.scoring_guidance: Meets when the learner preserves use testing or maintainability evidence when judging an implementation in a changed computing context or representation.

items.prompt: A bounded project changes domains, but the computing requirements, quality expectations, and reasoning structure still matter. Explain how the learner would avoid treating ap csa mastery as isolated java syntax recall.
items.expected_evidence: avoid treating AP CSA mastery as isolated Java syntax recall
items.scoring_guidance: Meets when the learner preserves avoid treating ap csa mastery as isolated java syntax recall in a changed computing context or representation.

items.prompt: After a delay, the learner is given a fresh AP-style synthesis or capstone-review case and must justify the design or critique using integrated computing reasoning. Show how they would reason across classes, methods, objects, and algorithms in one bounded program explanation.
items.expected_evidence: reason across classes, methods, objects, and algorithms in one bounded program explanation
items.scoring_guidance: Meets when the learner still demonstrates reason across classes, methods, objects, and algorithms in one bounded program explanation on a delayed recheck without a blocking recurrence.

items.prompt: Before closing ap csa program design, objects, and algorithmic implementation, run a short synthesis check on object-oriented and multi-component program reasoning and determine whether the learner is blocked by an upstream readiness gap rather than the current integration target.
items.expected_evidence: shows whether object-oriented and multi-component program reasoning is secure enough to support ap csa program design, objects, and algorithmic implementation
items.scoring_guidance: Meets when the learner response makes it possible to distinguish an upstream gap in object-oriented and multi-component program reasoning from a current-node issue in ap csa program design, objects, and algorithmic implementation.

items.prompt: Before closing ap csa program design, objects, and algorithmic implementation, run a short synthesis check on algorithm analysis, tradeoffs, and formalized problem solving and determine whether the learner is blocked by an upstream readiness gap rather than the current integration target.
items.expected_evidence: shows whether algorithm analysis, tradeoffs, and formalized problem solving is secure enough to support ap csa program design, objects, and algorithmic implementation
items.scoring_guidance: Meets when the learner response makes it possible to distinguish an upstream gap in algorithm analysis, tradeoffs, and formalized problem solving from a current-node issue in ap csa program design, objects, and algorithmic implementation.
items.suspected_knowledge_gap_if_incorrect: algorithm analysis, tradeoffs, and formalized problem solving

items.prompt: Before closing ap csa program design, objects, and algorithmic implementation, run a short synthesis check on refactoring, maintainability, and collaborative code change and determine whether the learner is blocked by an upstream readiness gap rather than the current integration target.
items.expected_evidence: shows whether refactoring, maintainability, and collaborative code change is secure enough to support ap csa program design, objects, and algorithmic implementation
items.scoring_guidance: Meets when the learner response makes it possible to distinguish an upstream gap in refactoring, maintainability, and collaborative code change from a current-node issue in ap csa program design, objects, and algorithmic implementation.
items.suspected_knowledge_gap_if_incorrect: refactoring, maintainability, and collaborative code change
